 {"id":"wa-brc7d","title":"[EPIC] asupersync Migration in FrankenTerm Crates — Replace smol/async-io/tokio","description":"# [EPIC] asupersync Migration in FrankenTerm Crates

## Why
FrankenTerm crates currently use smol (promise, mux, config) and async-io (async_ossl, wezterm-uds) for async. The project-wide strategy is to replace ALL async runtimes with asupersync (wa-e34d9). This epic covers the FrankenTerm-specific crate migrations.

## Context
- smol is used in: promise (core), mux (spawning), config (async config reload), codec (async PDU I/O)
- async-io is used in: async_ossl (OpenSSL async wrapper), wezterm-uds (Unix domain sockets)
- wezterm-ssh uses tokio via libssh2-sys — this is the hardest migration since libssh2 is C
- asupersync provides: Cx (capability context), structured concurrency, backpressure, cancellation

## Migration order
1. promise crate (foundation — other crates depend on it)
2. codec crate (PDU I/O — used by mux)
3. async_ossl + wezterm-uds (I/O primitives)
4. config crate (async config reload)
5. mux crate (spawning, domain management)
6. wezterm-ssh (hardest — C library async integration)

## Depends on
- wa-e34d9: asupersync must be ready (foundation + Cx infrastructure)
- wa-2umk2: FrankenTerm must be in-tree (we're modifying these crates)

## Strategy: feature-flagged dual-runtime
Like the Lua strategy, use feature flags during migration:
- `async-smol` (default initially): existing smol code
- `async-asupersync`: new asupersync code
- Once all crates migrated and tested, flip default and eventually remove smol","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:36.684463Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.163766Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d","depends_on_id":"wa-e34d9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.062387Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d","depends_on_id":"wa-2umk2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.163683Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.1","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in promise crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm promise crate

## Why
The promise crate (frankenterm/promise/) is the async foundation for all FrankenTerm crates. It provides `SpawnFunc`, `Promise<T>`, and async task spawning. Currently uses smol's block_on and spawn. Must be migrated first because mux, config, and codec depend on it.

## Current state
- frankenterm/promise/Cargo.toml: depends on smol
- frankenterm/promise/src/lib.rs: uses smol::block_on, smol::spawn
- frankenterm/promise/src/spawn.rs: SpawnFunc wraps smol executor

## What to change
1. Replace `smol::block_on` with `asupersync::block_on` (or Cx-based equivalent)
2. Replace `smol::spawn` with asupersync structured concurrency
3. Replace `smol::Timer` with asupersync timer
4. Feature flag: `async-smol` (default) vs `async-asupersync`
5. Both paths must produce identical behavior

## Key consideration
The promise crate's API must remain stable — it's used by every other FrankenTerm crate. The migration is internal implementation, not API change.

## Testing
- All existing promise tests pass with both feature flags
- Promise resolution works correctly with asupersync executor
- Cancellation behavior is preserved
- No leaked tasks after scope exit

## Test framework requirements

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent promise resolution
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent resolution of multiple promises never produces lost wakeups
- Promise::poll from multiple threads is linearizable (only one thread sees Ready)
- Cancellation of a promise while another thread resolves it never panics or leaks
- SpawnFunc task completion is visible to all waiters in bounded time

### criterion benchmarks — promise creation/resolution
Benchmark with criterion:
- Promise<T> creation time (target: <100ns per promise)
- Promise resolution (set value + wake) latency (target: <100ns)
- SpawnFunc overhead vs raw executor spawn (target: <2x overhead)
- Throughput: promises created and resolved per second (target: >10M/sec)

### proptest — promise state machine transitions
Use proptest to generate arbitrary sequences of promise operations (create, poll, resolve, cancel, drop) and verify:
- A promise is resolved at most once (no double-resolution)
- Polling a resolved promise always returns Ready with the correct value
- Dropping a promise before resolution does not leak the pending task
- State transitions follow the expected FSM: Pending -> {Resolved, Cancelled}","notes":"Added optional async-asupersync feature + block_on implementation in promise crate (commit 7f269ee).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:53.225259Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:56:05.272479-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:56:05.272482-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.1","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:53.225259Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.2","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in codec crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm codec crate

## Why
The codec crate (frankenterm/codec/) handles PDU serialization and async I/O for the mux wire protocol. Uses smol for async read/write. Must be migrated before mux.

## Current state
- Uses smol::io::AsyncRead/AsyncWrite traits
- PDU framing with async read/write
- Compression (zstd) in the I/O pipeline

## What to change
1. Replace smol async I/O traits with asupersync equivalents
2. Update async read/write implementations
3. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`
4. Ensure PDU framing is identical regardless of runtime

## Testing
- PDU round-trip tests pass with both runtimes
- Compression/decompression works correctly
- Large PDU handling (>64KB) works
- Connection drop handling is correct

## Test framework requirements

### criterion benchmarks — encode/decode throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- PDU encode throughput for typical message sizes (target: >500MB/s)
- PDU decode throughput for typical message sizes (target: >500MB/s)
- Compression ratio and throughput for zstd pipeline (target: >200MB/s with >2x compression)
- Large PDU handling (64KB, 256KB, 1MB) encode/decode latency
- Async framing overhead vs synchronous encode/decode

### proptest — codec roundtrip invariants
Use proptest to generate arbitrary PDU payloads and verify:
- encode followed by decode produces the original PDU (roundtrip identity: encode->decode = identity)
- Corrupted bytes in the stream produce Err, never panics or garbage data
- Partial reads (incomplete frames) are handled correctly and resume after more data arrives
- All PDU type variants round-trip correctly, including edge cases (empty payload, max-size payload)
- Compression is transparent: compressed and uncompressed PDUs decode identically","notes":"Closed via commit e2dda5d (2026-02-11): frankenterm/codec supports mutually-exclusive features async-smol (default) and async-asupersync with compile-time guards; async leb128 reader uses read_exact for asupersync compatibility; workspace chrono enables serde so mux builds under codec checks. Verified: cargo fmt -p codec --check; cargo check, clippy, and test for codec in both feature modes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:48:46.819729-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:48:46.819731-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:48.980803Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
-{"id":"wa-brc7d.3","title":"Replace async-io with asupersync in async_ossl and wezterm-uds","description":"# Replace async-io in async_ossl and wezterm-uds

## Why
These two crates provide async I/O primitives used by the mux and SSH systems:
- async_ossl: Async OpenSSL wrapper (TLS for mux connections)
- wezterm-uds: Async Unix domain socket handling

## Current state
- async_ossl/src/lib.rs: Uses async-io for polling OpenSSL BIO
- wezterm-uds/src/lib.rs: Uses async-io for Unix socket polling

## What to change
1. Replace async-io polling with asupersync I/O polling (Cx-based)
2. OpenSSL BIO integration: map SSL_read/SSL_write to asupersync futures
3. Unix socket: map connect/accept/read/write to asupersync
4. Feature flag both crates: `async-io` vs `async-asupersync`

## Key consideration
These crates deal with raw file descriptors and system calls. The asupersync I/O reactor must support registering arbitrary FDs for read/write readiness, similar to what async-io provides via Async<T>.

## macOS platform note
On macOS, the I/O notification backend must use **kqueue** instead of Linux's epoll. The asupersync I/O reactor should abstract over this via a platform-specific poller:
- Linux: epoll_create1 / epoll_ctl / epoll_wait
- macOS/BSD: kqueue / kevent
- Both async_ossl and wezterm-uds must be tested on macOS to ensure kqueue-based FD registration works correctly for OpenSSL BIOs and Unix domain sockets
- Note: macOS Unix domain socket paths are limited to 104 bytes (vs 108 on Linux)

## Testing
- TLS handshake works with asupersync
- Unix socket connection and data transfer works
- Verify no FD leaks after connection close
- Test with real mux server connection

## Test framework requirements

### criterion benchmarks — I/O throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- TLS handshake latency with asupersync vs async-io baseline (target: <5ms)
- Unix domain socket throughput (target: >1GB/s for local IPC)
- OpenSSL BIO read/write throughput through async wrapper (target: >500MB/s)
- FD registration/deregistration overhead in the I/O reactor

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent I/O operations
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent TLS connections never deadlock on BIO polling
- Concurrent Unix socket accept + read/write is linearizable
- FD close during pending read/write produces clean errors, no use-after-close
- Multiple tasks sharing an I/O reactor do not starve each other","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:10.599664Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:14.257695Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.3","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:10.599664Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-brc7d.4","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in config crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm config crate

## Why
The config crate uses smol for async config file watching and reload. 

## What to change
1. Replace smol file watcher with asupersync equivalent
2. Replace smol timer for debounced reload
3. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`
4. Config reload callback mechanism must work with both runtimes

## Testing
- Config file modification triggers reload with both runtimes
- Debounce works correctly (rapid changes → single reload)
- Config errors don't crash the watcher

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — config parsing roundtrips
Use proptest to generate arbitrary config structures (with valid and edge-case field values) and verify:
- Serialize to TOML then parse back produces the original config (roundtrip identity)
- Invalid field values produce descriptive errors, never panics
- Unknown keys are ignored (forward compatibility for config evolution)
- Default values are correctly applied for missing fields
- Nested config sections (colors, fonts, keybindings) all round-trip correctly

### criterion benchmarks — config hot-reload latency
Benchmark with criterion:
- Config file parse time for typical frankenterm.toml (target: <1ms)
- Hot-reload end-to-end latency: file change detection to config applied (target: <50ms)
- Debounce timer accuracy: verify rapid changes coalesce correctly
- Config diff computation: detect which fields changed (target: <100us)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:17.393741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:21.996251Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.4","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:17.393741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.4","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.175457Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.5","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in mux crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm mux crate

## Why
The mux crate is the largest consumer of smol. It uses async for: domain management, pane I/O, tab management, mux server communication, and task spawning. This is the most complex migration.

## Current state
- Uses smol::spawn for background tasks
- Uses smol channels for pane I/O
- Uses smol::Timer for timeouts
- Uses promise crate (which wraps smol)
- Domain trait has async methods

## What to change
1. Replace all smol::spawn with asupersync structured concurrency
2. Replace smol channels with asupersync channels
3. Replace smol::Timer with asupersync time (via Cx)
4. Update Domain trait async methods for asupersync
5. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`

## Dependencies
- promise crate must be migrated first (wa-brc7d.1)
- codec crate must be migrated first (wa-brc7d.2)
- async_ossl must be migrated for TLS domains (wa-brc7d.3)

## Testing
- All mux tests pass with both runtimes
- Domain connection/disconnection works
- Pane I/O (read/write) works
- Tab creation/destruction works
- Concurrent multi-pane operation works
- No task leaks after tab close

## Test framework requirements

### LabRuntime DPOR — mux concurrency
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent pane read/write across multiple tabs never deadlocks
- Domain disconnect during active pane I/O produces clean errors, no orphaned tasks
- Tab close while pane I/O is in-flight cancels all pending operations
- Channel send/receive ordering is preserved under concurrent multi-producer scenarios
- Task spawning and structured concurrency scopes do not leak tasks on cancellation

### criterion benchmarks — mux operation throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- Pane I/O throughput: bytes read/written per second through mux layer (target: >500MB/s local)
- Tab creation/destruction latency (target: <500us per tab)
- Domain connection establishment latency (target: <10ms local, <100ms remote)
- Channel throughput for pane I/O pipeline (target: >1M messages/sec)

### Loom model checking — shared state
Use Loom to exhaustively verify the mux crate's shared state synchronization:
- Arc<Mutex<Tab>> access patterns are deadlock-free
- Domain registry (shared HashMap) concurrent access is safe
- Pane ID generation (AtomicU64) is monotonic and unique under contention
- No data races in the pane I/O buffer management

## Cross-references
- **wa-2dd4s.5** — FrankenMux integration: the mux crate provides the async runtime foundation that FrankenMux's unified tab model builds upon; this migration must complete before FrankenMux integration can use asupersync
- **wa-brc7d.1** — promise crate migration: must complete first as mux depends on promise
- **wa-brc7d.2** — codec crate migration: must complete first as mux depends on codec for wire protocol
- **wa-brc7d.3** — async_ossl/wezterm-uds migration: must complete first for TLS domain connections","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:27.387986Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:38.117734Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:27.387986Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.297251Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.402757Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.515142Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.6","title":"Replace tokio refs in wezterm-ssh with asupersync","description":"# Replace tokio in wezterm-ssh with asupersync

## Why
wezterm-ssh is the hardest migration. It wraps libssh2 (C library) with Rust async via tokio. The C library has its own event loop expectations.

## Current state
- Uses tokio::spawn, tokio::time, tokio channels
- SSH session runs on dedicated tokio thread
- libssh2 session callbacks drive async I/O
- Complex state machine for SSH connection lifecycle

## What to change
1. Replace tokio primitives with asupersync equivalents
2. The dedicated SSH thread may need to become an asupersync task
3. libssh2 polling integration: need asupersync I/O reactor FD registration
4. Feature flag: `async-tokio` vs `async-asupersync`

## Key risk
libssh2 expects specific threading and polling behavior. Changing the async runtime may break subtle timing assumptions. Extensive testing needed.

## Testing
- SSH connection to real server works
- SCP file transfer works
- Port forwarding works
- Connection timeout works
- Graceful disconnect works
- Stress test: 10 concurrent SSH connections

## Test framework requirements

### criterion benchmarks — SSH channel throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- SSH channel data throughput (target: >100MB/s over localhost)
- SSH session establishment latency (target: <500ms including key exchange)
- SCP file transfer throughput for various file sizes (1KB, 1MB, 100MB)
- Channel multiplex overhead: N channels over 1 SSH connection vs N separate connections

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent SSH sessions
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent SSH session establishment never deadlocks on libssh2's internal locks
- Concurrent channel open/close on the same session is linearizable
- Session disconnect during active channel I/O produces clean errors in all channels
- Multiple SSH sessions sharing the asupersync I/O reactor do not starve each other
- libssh2 callback re-entrancy is safe under asupersync's task scheduling","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:36.727778Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:48.824625Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.6","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:36.727778Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.6","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.616576Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.6","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.722958Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
@@ -624,7 +624,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-dwa","title":"[EPIC] E2E Test Harness: Scripted scenarios, detailed logging, CI integration","description":"# [EPIC] E2E Test Harness

## Mission
Provide a **comprehensive E2E testing framework** with scripted scenarios, detailed logging, and CI integration that validates wa works correctly in real-world conditions.

## Why This Matters
Unit tests validate components in isolation. E2E tests validate **the whole system works together**:
- WezTerm integration actually works
- Patterns detect real agent output
- Workflows execute correctly end-to-end
- Error recovery handles real failures

Without E2E tests, we ship bugs that only manifest in production.

## E2E Test Architecture

### Test Harness
```
tests/e2e/
├── harness/
│   ├── mod.rs           # Test harness library
│   ├── fixtures.rs      # Test fixtures and setup
│   ├── assertions.rs    # Custom assertions
│   └── logging.rs       # Detailed test logging
├── scenarios/
│   ├── basic_ingest.rs  # Basic text capture
│   ├── pattern_detection.rs
│   ├── workflow_execution.rs
│   ├── policy_enforcement.rs
│   └── error_recovery.rs
├── scripts/
│   ├── run_all.sh       # Run all E2E tests
│   ├── setup_wezterm.sh # Setup test WezTerm instance
│   └── cleanup.sh       # Cleanup after tests
└── fixtures/
    ├── codex_session.txt
    ├── claude_session.txt
    └── error_scenarios/
```

### Detailed Logging
Every E2E test produces detailed logs:
```
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.123Z] [INFO] === Starting E2E Test: workflow_execution ===
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.124Z] [DEBUG] Setting up test environment...
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.125Z] [DEBUG] Starting WezTerm mock pane...
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.200Z] [INFO] Pane created: id=99, title="test-codex"
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.201Z] [DEBUG] Injecting test output: "Usage limit reached..."
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.500Z] [INFO] Event detected: codex.usage_limit_reached
[2026-01-18T14:30:00.501Z] [DEBUG] Workflow started: handle_usage_limits
[2026-01-18T14:30:01.000Z] [INFO] Step 1/7 completed: exit_codex
[2026-01-18T14:30:02.000Z] [INFO] Step 2/7 completed: wait_for_summary
...
[2026-01-18T14:30:10.000Z] [INFO] Workflow completed successfully
[2026-01-18T14:30:10.001Z] [INFO] === E2E Test PASSED: workflow_execution (9.878s) ===
```

### Test Scenarios

#### 1. Basic Ingest
- Start wa watch
- Create mock pane with text
- Verify text captured in DB
- Verify FTS indexed

#### 2. Pattern Detection
- Inject known patterns into pane
- Verify events detected
- Verify event metadata correct
- Test false positive rejection

#### 3. Workflow Execution
- Trigger workflow via event
- Verify each step executes
- Verify final state correct
- Test workflow resume after restart

#### 4. Policy Enforcement
- Attempt blocked actions
- Verify policy denies correctly
- Test approval flow
- Verify audit trail

#### 5. Error Recovery
- Simulate WezTerm disconnect
- Verify graceful degradation
- Verify reconnection works
- Test circuit breaker behavior

### CI Integration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/e2e.yml
e2e-tests:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  services:
    wezterm:
      image: wezterm/wezterm:latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - name: Build wa
      run: cargo build --release
    - name: Run E2E tests
      run: ./tests/e2e/scripts/run_all.sh
      env:
        E2E_LOG_LEVEL: debug
        E2E_TIMEOUT: 300
    - name: Upload logs
      if: always()
      uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: e2e-logs
        path: tests/e2e/logs/
```

### Test Fixtures
Pre-recorded terminal sessions for reproducible testing:
```rust
struct TestFixture {
    name: &'static str,
    pane_title: &'static str,
    output_chunks: Vec<OutputChunk>,
    expected_events: Vec<ExpectedEvent>,
}

static CODEX_USAGE_LIMIT: TestFixture = TestFixture {
    name: "codex_usage_limit",
    pane_title: "codex @ /project",
    output_chunks: vec![
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 0, text: "Working on task..." },
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 5000, text: "\n\nYou've reached your usage limit.\n" },
        OutputChunk { delay_ms: 100, text: "Daily limit resets in 4 hours.\n" },
    ],
    expected_events: vec![
        ExpectedEvent { rule_id: "codex.usage_limit_reached", .. },
    ],
};
```

## Success Criteria
- E2E tests cover all major user journeys
- Tests produce detailed, actionable logs
- CI runs E2E tests on every PR
- Test failures are reproducible locally
- Test runtime < 5 minutes for full suite

## Testing
- Harness unit tests: fixture replay ordering, log writer, and custom assertions.
- Integration tests: scenario registry + harness lifecycle (setup/teardown).
- E2E: a minimal smoke scenario that exercises the harness itself and produces an artifact bundle.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Harness can run at least the five core scenarios with deterministic ordering.
- Each scenario emits a structured log with timestamps and a PASS/FAIL summary.
- CI runs the harness and uploads artifacts for every run.
- Failures are reproducible locally using the same fixture set.

-{"id":"wa-e34d9","title":"Epic: Replace tokio async runtime with asupersync","description":"# Epic: Replace tokio async runtime with asupersync

## Background
FrankenTerm currently uses tokio 1.49.0 (full features) as its async runtime, with ~1,090 async patterns across 61 files. The asupersync library (https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/asupersync) is a spec-first, cancel-correct, capability-secure Rust async runtime that replaces tokio entirely.

## Why asupersync?
1. **Structured concurrency** - Every spawned task belongs to a Scope; no orphan tasks possible
2. **Cancel-correctness** - Multi-phase cancellation protocol (Request→Drain→Finalize) vs tokio's silent drop
3. **Capability security** - All effects gated through Cx context token; no ambient authority
4. **Four-valued Outcome<T,E>** - Ok/Err/Cancelled/Panicked vs Result's Ok/Err
5. **Two-phase channel ops** - Reserve/Commit pattern eliminates "message lost on cancellation" bugs
6. **Deterministic testing** - LabRuntime with virtual time, seed-controlled scheduling, DPOR exploration
7. **Built-in networking** - TCP/UDP/TLS/HTTP/gRPC without tokio transitive deps

## Scope
Replace ALL tokio usage across the codebase:
- Core runtime and task spawning → asupersync Scope/Cx
- Sync primitives (Mutex, RwLock, Semaphore) → asupersync sync
- Channels (mpsc, watch, broadcast) → asupersync channels
- Network I/O (UnixStream, TcpListener) → asupersync net
- Time (sleep, timeout) → asupersync via Cx
- select! macro → asupersync combinators
- tokio-rustls → asupersync TLS
- reqwest → asupersync HTTP client
- Test infrastructure → LabRuntime

## Key Migration Patterns
| Tokio | Asupersync |
|-------|------------|
| #[tokio::main] | RuntimeBuilder::new().build()?.block_on(...) |
| tokio::spawn(fut) | scope.spawn(fut) inside cx.region() |
| tokio::select! | asupersync::combinator::select/race |
| tokio::time::sleep | cx.sleep(duration) |
| tokio::sync::mpsc | asupersync::channel::mpsc (reserve/commit) |
| tokio::sync::Mutex | asupersync::sync::Mutex (guard obligation) |
| Result<T,E> | Outcome<T,E> (four-valued) |
| Drop cancellation | cx.checkpoint() explicit cancellation points |

## Critical Consideration
All async functions gain a `cx: &mut Cx` parameter. This is the most pervasive change - it threads through the entire call graph. Plan function signature changes carefully to minimize churn.

## Estimated Scale
- 61 files with async code
- ~1,090 async pattern instances
- ~586 network I/O instances
- Core modules: pool.rs (554 LOC), mux_pool.rs (583 LOC), mux_client.rs (997 LOC), runtime.rs (~500 LOC), ipc.rs (~400 LOC), tailer.rs (~600 LOC), native_events.rs (~400 LOC)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:47:01.064371Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T03:47:01.064371Z"}

 {"id":"wa-e6pq","title":"Layout restoration engine — recreate window/tab/split topology from snapshot","description":"## Goal
Implement the layout restoration engine that recreates the exact window/tab/pane split topology from a MuxSnapshot, positioning panes in the same arrangement they had before the mux server restart.

## Background & Motivation
After a mux server restart, all windows, tabs, and split panes are gone. The restoration engine must recreate the exact same layout using WezTerm CLI commands (wezterm cli spawn, wezterm cli split-pane). This is the visual foundation — users should see the same window arrangement they had before.

WezTerm's resurrect.wezterm plugin does approximate layout restoration via Lua but has limitations: it can't restore exact split ratios, and it struggles with complex nested splits. Our approach uses the vendored mux client and exact pane geometry data captured in the MuxSnapshot.

## Technical Design

### Struct Definition
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_layout.rs
pub struct LayoutRestorer {
    mux_client: Arc<DirectMuxClient>,
    config: RestoreConfig,
}

pub struct RestoreConfig {
    pub restore_working_dirs: bool,  // default: true
    pub restore_split_ratios: bool,  // default: true
    pub restore_window_positions: bool, // default: false (OS may differ)
}

// Maps old pane IDs to new pane IDs after restoration
pub type PaneIdMap = HashMap<PaneId, PaneId>;
```

### Core Algorithm
1. For each window in the snapshot:
   a. Create a new window (wezterm cli spawn --new-window)
   b. Record the new window_id
2. For each tab in the window:
   a. If not the first tab, create new tab (wezterm cli spawn-tab)
   b. Record the new tab_id
3. For the pane tree within each tab:
   a. Use recursive descent on the PaneNode tree
   b. For Split nodes: create split-pane with correct direction (horizontal/vertical)
   c. For Leaf nodes: record the pane_id mapping (old -> new)
   d. Adjust split ratios using resize commands

### Recursive Split Restoration
```rust
async fn restore_pane_tree(
    &self,
    node: &PaneNode,
    parent_pane_id: PaneId,
    id_map: &mut PaneIdMap,
) -> Result<PaneId> {
    match node {
        PaneNode::Leaf { pane_id, cwd, .. } => {
            id_map.insert(*pane_id, parent_pane_id);
            if self.config.restore_working_dirs {
                // Set working directory via shell command
                self.set_cwd(parent_pane_id, cwd).await?;
            }
            Ok(parent_pane_id)
        }
        PaneNode::Split { direction, children, ratio, .. } => {
            // First child uses the existing pane
            let first = self.restore_pane_tree(&children[0], parent_pane_id, id_map).await?;
            // Second child gets a new split pane
            let new_pane = self.create_split(parent_pane_id, *direction).await?;
            let second = self.restore_pane_tree(&children[1], new_pane, id_map).await?;
            // Adjust split ratio
            if self.config.restore_split_ratios {
                self.adjust_ratio(first, second, *ratio).await?;
            }
            Ok(first)
        }
    }
}
```

## Integration Points
- **MuxSnapshot schema** (bd-cuz): Reads PaneNode tree structure
- **DirectMuxClient**: Uses spawn, split-pane, and resize PDUs
- **WeztermClient** (wezterm.rs): Fallback to CLI for operations not in mux protocol
- **PaneIdMap**: Returned to caller for use by scrollback injection and process re-launch

## Key Files to Create/Modify
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/restore_layout.rs
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/mod.rs (add module)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs (may need spawn/split PDU support)

## Edge Cases
- Window manager may not honor exact window positions (especially across restarts)
- Split ratios may need multiple resize iterations to converge
- If mux server has existing panes, restoration should create in addition (not replace)
- Tab titles should be restored from snapshot metadata

## Dependencies
- bd-cuz: MuxSnapshot schema (PaneNode tree definition)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Single window with multiple tabs restores correctly
- Horizontal and vertical splits restore with correct topology
- Nested splits (3+ levels deep) restore correctly
- Split ratios are approximately correct (within 5% of original)
- Working directories are set correctly for each pane
- PaneIdMap correctly maps all old pane IDs to new ones
- Handles graceful errors (e.g., if a split fails, continues with remaining)

## Estimated Effort
2-3 hours implementation, 1 hour testing

## Test Requirements

### Property-Based Testing (proptest)
Add proptest cases in `crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_layout_restore.rs`:
- **Layout roundtrip invariant**: For any arbitrary layout tree, `save(restore(save(layout))) == save(layout)` — saving a restored layout must produce an identical snapshot to the original. This is the fundamental correctness property. Strategy: `arb_layout_tree()` generates random PaneNode trees with 1-50 panes, random split directions, and random ratios in [0.1, 0.9].
- **PaneIdMap completeness**: For any layout tree, after restoration the PaneIdMap must contain exactly one entry for every leaf pane in the original tree (no missing mappings, no extra mappings).
- **Topology preservation**: For any layout tree, the structural shape (split directions, nesting depth, child ordering) of the restored layout must exactly match the original, regardless of specific pane IDs.
- **Split ratio fidelity**: For any layout tree with split ratios, restored ratios must be within 5% of originals. Strategy: `arb_ratio()` generates f64 in [0.1, 0.9].

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add benchmarks in `crates/wa-core/benches/layout_restore.rs`:
- `bench_restore_50_pane_layout`: Restore a 50-pane complex layout (10 tabs, mixed splits), target <500ms
- `bench_restore_single_tab_deep_splits`: Restore a single tab with 10-level deep nested splits
- `bench_pane_id_map_construction`: PaneIdMap construction for 200 panes, target <1ms

## Cross-References
- **wa-2dd4s.3** (Zellij swap layouts): Zellij-style swap layouts provide named layout presets that can be toggled. The layout restoration engine should be aware of swap layout metadata in snapshots so that restored sessions can resume swap-layout cycling from where the user left off.
- **wa-2dd4s.2** (Floating panes): Floating (overlay) panes are not part of the split tree — they exist as separate overlay layers. The layout restoration engine must handle floating pane metadata separately from the recursive split restoration, restoring them as overlays at their original positions and sizes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:31:53.823029Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T22:09:37.599242-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T22:09:37.599242-05:00","close_reason":"Layout restoration engine: LayoutRestorer with recursive split tree restoration, PaneIdMap, CWD normalization, file:// URI handling, configurable split ratios, continue-on-error. 27 tests.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-e6pq","depends_on_id":"wa-rsaf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:01.731154Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-e6pq","depends_on_id":"wa-rbvl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:19.671209Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-e6pq","depends_on_id":"wa-cuz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:19.671209Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-e6pq","depends_on_id":"wa-ooje","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:01:51.701854Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-e6pq","depends_on_id":"wa-2t2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T20:01:51.701854Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-eiq5","title":"Tests for handle_compaction (synthetic detection → one send → step logs)","description":"# Task: handle_compaction tests

## Goal
Ensure compaction handling doesn’t regress.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests (fixture-first):
  - given a synthetic compaction detection and pane state `PromptActive`:
    - workflow runs
    - sends exactly once
    - step logs show completion
  - if pane is `AltScreen`:
    - workflow aborts/denies
  - if compaction anchor is not present in tail:
    - workflow aborts safely (stale detection)

- Logging assertions:
  - failures report which guard/step failed (no “mystery timeouts”)

- E2E linkage:
  - ensure these tests align with the end-to-end scenario in `wa-4vx.10.8` (same prompts/markers).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests cover both the success path and the primary safety abort paths.
- Failures produce actionable diffs (which guard failed, which step did not match).
